Let \(m=p_1 ^{a_1} p_2 ^{a_2} \ldots p_k ^{a_k}\). Solving \(f(x) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod m\) is equivalent to finding the simultaneous solutions to the system of congruences:

$$ f(x) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod p_i ^{a_i}, \quad i=1,2,\ldots,k $$

Once the solutions of each of the \(k\) congruences modulo \(p_i^{a_i}\) is known, the solutions of the congruence modulo \(m\) can be found by the Chinese remainder theorem.

Since \(200 = 2^3 * 5^2\) We can reduce the problem by finding the solutions of \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 2^3\) and \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5^2\):

$$\begin{gather} 2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 8 \\ 2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \end{gather}$$

Or:

$$\begin{gather} x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8 \\ x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 25 \end{gather}$$

Since \(2x^2\) is always even, \(2x^2 + 7\) is odd. For \(x(2x^2 + 7)\) to be congruent to 4 modulo 8, \(x\) has to be even so \(x(2x^2 + 7)\) could be even. If \(x â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 8\), then \(x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 8\). If \(x â‰ˇ 2 \bmod 8\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 + 7 â‰ˇ 7 \bmod 8 \\ x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 6 \bmod 8 \end{gather}$$

If \(x â‰ˇ 6 \bmod 8\), then:

$$\begin{gather} x^2 â‰ˇ 36 â‰ˇ 4 \\ 2x^2 + 7 â‰ˇ 7 \bmod 8 \\ x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 42 â‰ˇ 2 \bmod 8 \end{gather}$$

Lastly, if \(x â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 + 7 â‰ˇ 7 \bmod 8 \\ x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8 \end{gather}$$

So the solution to \(x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8\) is \(x â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8\).

Now let's focus on \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25\). If \(25 | 2x^3 + 7x - 4\), then \(5 | 2x^3 + 7x - 4\), so that means \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5\). Every solution of \(x\) where \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25\) is also a solution to \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5\) (the reverse is not necessarily true). The contrapositive is that any value of \(x\) that does not solve \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5\) also doesn't solve \(2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25\). Rearranging:

$$\begin{gather} x(2x^2 + 7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

If \(xâ‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5\), then \(x(2x^2 +7) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5\).

If \(xâ‰ˇ 1 \bmod 5\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 +7 â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 5 \\ x(2x^2 +7) â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

If \(xâ‰ˇ 2 \bmod 5\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 +7 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5 \\ x(2x^2 +7) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

If \(xâ‰ˇ 3 \bmod 5\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 +7 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5 \\ x(2x^2 +7) â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

If \(xâ‰ˇ 4 \bmod 5\), then:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^2 +7 â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 5 \\ x(2x^2 +7) â‰ˇ 1 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

That means if \(x\) is congruent to either 0, 1, 2 or 3 modulo 5 then there is no solution, so \(x\) has to be congruent to 1 modulo 5. Therefore the solution modulo 25 is \(5k+1\) for some integer \(k\). This means:

$$\begin{gather} 2x^3 + 7x - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \\ 2(5k+1)^3 + 7(5k+1) - 4 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \\ 2(125k^3 + 3(25)k^2 + 3(5)k + 1) + 35k+7 â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 25 \\ 250k^3 + 150k^2 + 30k + 2 + 35k+7 â‰ˇ 4 \\ 250k^3 + 150k^2 + 65k +5 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \\ 0 + 0 + 15k +5 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \\ 15k +5 â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 25 \\ 3k +1â‰ˇ 0 \bmod 5 \end{gather}$$

When manually checking different values of \(k\), we find that only \(k=3\) works. Therefore \(x=5(3)+1=16\) modulo 25.

If \(x â‰ˇ 4 \bmod 8\) and \(x â‰ˇ 16 \bmod 25\), then we can use the Chinese remainder theorem to find that \(x â‰ˇ 116 \bmod 200\).
